question: The director of the romantic comedy "Big Stone Gap" is based in what New York city?
prediction:
Answer: Greenwich Village, New York City  
Citations: [Doc 1], [Doc 4]

retrieved_docs.title: Big Stone Gap (film)
retrieved_docs.text: Big Stone Gap is a 2014 American drama romantic comedy film written and directed by Adriana Trigiani and produced by Donna Gigliotti for Altar Identity Studios, a subsidiary of Media Society.  Based on Trigiani's 2000 best-selling novel of the same name, the story is set in the actual Virginia town of Big Stone Gap circa 1970s.  The film had its world premiere at the Virginia Film Festival on November 6, 2014.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinton, Minnesota
retrieved_docs.text: Clinton is a city in Big Stone County, Minnesota, United States.  The city was named for New York Governor DeWitt Clinton.  The population was 449 at the 2010 census.

retrieved_docs.title: Nola (film)
retrieved_docs.text: Nola is a 2003 American romantic comedy film written and directed by Alan Hruska.  It depicts the struggle of a young woman trying to survive in New York City while looking for her birth father.  It premiered in New York City on July 23, 2004.

retrieved_docs.title: Adriana Trigiani
retrieved_docs.text: Adriana Trigiani is an Italian American best-selling author of sixteen books, television writer, film director, and entrepreneur based in Greenwich Village, New York City.  Trigiani has published a novel a year since 2000.

retrieved_docs.title: Kingston Morning
retrieved_docs.text: Kingston Morning is Dave Eggar's 4th solo release recorded in Brooklyn, New York; Kingston, Jamaica; and Big Stone Gap, Virginia; and released by Domo Records.  "Itsbynne Reel" was nominated at the 53rd Grammy Awards for "Best Instrumental Arrangement".

question: Are Giuseppe Verdi and Ambroise Thomas both Opera composers ?
prediction:
Answer: Yes, both are opera composers.  
Citations: [Doc 1], [Doc 2]

retrieved_docs.title: Ambroise Thomas
retrieved_docs.text: Charles Louis Ambroise Thomas (5 August 1811 ¨C 12 February 1896) was a French composer, best known for his operas "Mignon" (1866) and "Hamlet" (1868, after Shakespeare) and as Director of the Conservatoire de Paris from 1871 till his death.

retrieved_docs.title: Giuseppe Verdi
retrieved_docs.text: Giuseppe Fortunino Francesco Verdi (] ; 9 or 10 October 1813 ¨C 27 January 1901) was an Italian opera composer.

retrieved_docs.title: Verdi, the King of Melody
retrieved_docs.text: Giuseppe Verdi, released theatrically in the USA as The Life and Music of Giuseppe Verdi and on video as Verdi, the King of Melody, is a 1953 Italian biographical film starring Pierre Cressoy and directed by Raffaello Matarazzo.  It is based on adult life events of the composer Giuseppe Verdi.  The film was a commercial success, grossing over 957 million lire at the Italian box office.

retrieved_docs.title: Giuseppe Verdi (film)
retrieved_docs.text: Giuseppe Verdi is a 1938 Italian biographical film directed by Carmine Gallone and starring Fosco Giachetti, Gaby Morlay and Germana Paolieri.  The film portrays the life of the composer Giuseppe Verdi (1813-1901).  The casting of Giachetti as Verdi was intended to emphasise the composer's patriotism, as he had recently played patriotic roles in films such as "The White Squadron".  The film was made at the Cinecitt¨¤ Studios in Rome.  The film is also known by the alternative title The Life of Giuseppe Verdi.

retrieved_docs.title: List of major opera composers
retrieved_docs.text: This list provides a guide to opera composers, as determined by their presence on a majority of compiled lists of significant opera composers.  (See the "Lists Consulted" section for full details.)  The composers run from Jacopo Peri, who wrote the first ever opera in late 16th century Italy, to John Adams, one of the leading figures in the contemporary operatic world.  The brief accompanying notes offer an explanation as to why each composer has been considered major.  Also included is a section about major women opera composers, compiled from the same lists.  For an introduction to operatic history, see opera.  The organisation of the list is by birthdate.

retrieved_docs.title: 2011¨C12 VCU Rams men's basketball team
retrieved_docs.text: The 2011¨C12 VCU Rams men's basketball team represented Virginia Commonwealth University during the 2011¨C12 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.  It was the 44th season of the University fielding a men's basketball program.  Led by third-year head coach Shaka Smart, the Rams were coming off a season marked by a run to the Final Four.  Expected to finish lower in the CAA regular season standings, the Rams finished as regular season runners-up with a 15¨C3 conference record, before winning the 2012 CAA Men's Basketball Championship against Drexel, 59¨C56, earning their 11th ever berth into the NCAA Tournament.  It was also the first season since 1984¨C85 that the Rams consecutively qualified for the NCAA Tournament.

retrieved_docs.title: 2009¨C10 VCU Rams men's basketball team
retrieved_docs.text: The 2009¨C10 VCU Rams men's basketball team represented Virginia Commonwealth University during the 2009¨C10 college basketball season.  This was head coach Shaka Smart's first season at VCU.  The Rams compete in the Colonial Athletic Association and played their home games at Stuart C. Siegel Center.  They finished the season 27¨C9, 11¨C7 in CAA play and lost in the semifinals of the 2010 CAA Men's Basketball Tournament.  They were champions of the 2010 College Basketball Invitational.

retrieved_docs.title: 2012¨C13 VCU Rams men's basketball team
retrieved_docs.text: The 2012¨C13 VCU Rams men's basketball team represented Virginia Commonwealth University during the 2012¨C13 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.  It was the 45th season of the University fielding a men's basketball program.  Led by fourth-year head coach Shaka Smart, they played their home games at the Stuart C. Siegel Center.  This was the Rams inaugural season in the Atlantic 10 Conference (A10), after spending the past 17 years in the Colonial Athletic Association (CAA).  They finished the season 27¨C9, 12¨C4 in A10 play to finish in second place.  They advanced to the championship game of the 2013 Atlantic 10 Tournament where they lost to Saint Louis.  They received an at-large bid to the 2013 NCAA Tournament, their third straight NCAA Tournament appearance, where they defeated Akron in the first round before losing in the third round to Michigan.

retrieved_docs.title: 1995¨C96 VCU Rams men's basketball team
retrieved_docs.text: The 1995¨C96 VCU Rams men's basketball team represented Virginia Commonwealth University during the 1995¨C96 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.  It was the 28th season of the University fielding a men's basketball program, and the program's first season in the Colonial Athletic Association, after previously playing in the Metro Conference.  The Rams were coached by 7th year head coach, Sonny Smith.

retrieved_docs.title: 2015¨C16 VCU Rams men's basketball team
retrieved_docs.text: The 2015¨C16 VCU Rams men's basketball team represented Virginia Commonwealth University during the 2015¨C16 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.  It was the 48th season of the University fielding a men's basketball program.  The program was led by Will Wade, who was leading the program for his first season after previously coaching Chattanooga.  Wade replaced former head coach, Shaka Smart, who resigned from VCU for the vacancy at Texas.
